name: ChatGPT
tagline: The world's most popular AI assistant
bestFor: General AI chat, research, writing, coding
whoShouldNotUse: Users who primarily work with very long documents — Claude's 1M token context window is dramatically better for full reports, legal texts, and entire codebases. Also not ideal if you never want to deal with usage limits on free tier.

name: Claude
tagline: The AI assistant built for safety and long-form reasoning
whoShouldNotUse: Developers who rely on native code execution, image generation (DALL-E), or a large plugin ecosystem. If you code daily and need an AI that can run Python in the chat, ChatGPT's code interpreter is a better fit.

name: Notion
tagline: The all-in-one workspace for notes, docs, and databases
whoShouldNotUse: Teams of 50+ who need serious project management — Gantt charts, workload views, resource allocation, and cross-team dependency tracking. Notion's PM capabilities are too lightweight for enterprise project offices.

name: Midjourney
tagline: The leading AI image generator for creative professionals
whoShouldNotUse: Anyone creating images for commercial client work, advertising campaigns, or products where legal clearance matters. Midjourney's commercial licensing is legally murky — Adobe Firefly is the only commercially safe AI image tool.

name: ClickUp
tagline: The most feature-rich project management tool
whoShouldNotUse: Software engineering teams that live in their issue tracker all day. The feature overload and slower UI will frustrate developers — Linear is significantly faster and better designed for dev workflows. Don't use ClickUp as a Jira alternative for engineering teams.

name: Jasper
tagline: The AI writing tool built for marketing teams
whoShouldNotUse: Solo creators, freelancers, or teams producing content for themselves or one client. The $49/mo minimum and team-oriented features don't justify the cost at small scale — ChatGPT Plus at $20/mo does 80% of what Jasper does.

name: Copy.ai
tagline: AI writing for marketers and startups

name: Writesonic
tagline: Affordable AI writing for bloggers and marketers

name: Grammarly
tagline: The gold standard for grammar and writing clarity
whoShouldNotUse: Users who already pay for Claude Pro or ChatGPT Plus. The value overlap between Grammarly Premium ($30/mo) and a capable AI assistant is too high to justify both. Keep Grammarly Free as a passive backup — don't pay for Premium.

name: DALL-E 3
tagline: OpenAI's image generator, built into ChatGPT
pricing: Pay per use (API), included in ChatGPT Plus

name: Adobe Firefly
tagline: AI image generation built for Adobe Creative Cloud

name: Stable Diffusion
tagline: The open-source AI image generator for power users
pros: Highly customizable with LoRAs and fine-tuning
cons: Steep learning curve for best results

name: Obsidian
tagline: The privacy-first knowledge management tool

name: Mem
tagline: The AI-powered note-taking app that organizes itself
cons: Less robust than Notion or Obsidian

name: Logseq
tagline: The open-source knowledge base for outlining and research

name: Roam Research
tagline: The networked note-taking tool for serious researchers

name: Asana
tagline: The team project management tool trusted by enterprises
cons: Can be overwhelming for small teams

name: Monday.com
tagline: The visual project management platform for SMBs
cons: Can get expensive for large teams

name: Linear
tagline: The fastest issue tracker for software teams

name: Descript
tagline: Edit video like a document — the easiest video editor

name: Runway
tagline: AI-powered video creation and editing for creators
cons: Limited export options on free plan

name: Synthesia
tagline: Create AI avatar videos without cameras or actors

name: HeyGen
tagline: Realistic AI avatars for marketing and sales videos

name: Notion AI
tagline: AI writing assistant built directly into Notion

name: ChatGPT Team
tagline: ChatGPT for business teams with privacy controls
pros: Access to GPT-4o and latest models

name: Google Gemini
tagline: Google's flagship AI assistant, built into the Google ecosystem
bestFor: Google Workspace users, research, multimodal tasks
pros: Deep integration with Google Workspace (Docs, Gmail, Drive)
Multimodal by default — handles text, images, and code
Gemini Advanced is genuinely competitive with GPT-4o
cons: Privacy concerns for users avoiding Google data collection
Advanced features require Google One subscription
notGoodAt: Users who need privacy from big tech, or complex long-form document analysis
whoShouldNotUse: Users who don't use Google Workspace, or anyone with data privacy concerns about Google. Without Gmail, Docs, and Drive integration, Gemini loses its main advantage over ChatGPT and Claude.

name: Perplexity AI
tagline: The AI answer engine that cites its sources
bestFor: Research, fact-checking, real-time information with citations
pros: Cites sources for every answer — verifiable
Clean, focused interface with no fluff
Pro Search goes deeper on complex queries
cons: Not a writing assistant — it answers questions, not drafts content
Citations can occasionally be outdated or misattributed
Less capable at creative or generative tasks
notGoodAt: Content creation, writing drafts, or anything beyond answering questions. Perplexity is a research tool, not an AI writing assistant.
whoShouldNotUse: Anyone looking for an AI writing assistant. Perplexity answers questions with cited sources — it doesn't write content, draft emails, or create. If you need to produce text rather than find information, use Claude or ChatGPT instead.

name: Canva AI
tagline: AI design tools built into the world's most popular design platform
bestFor: Social media graphics, presentations, and marketing materials
pros: Already inside Canva — no new tool to learn
Magic Design, Magic Write, and AI image generation in one place
Best for non-designers who need professional-looking output
cons: AI image quality doesn't match standalone generators like Midjourney
Pro subscription required for most AI features
Not suitable for photorealistic image generation
AI writing is basic compared to Claude or ChatGPT
notGoodAt: Photorealistic image generation or serious creative artwork. For those use cases, Midjourney or Stable Diffusion are far superior.
whoShouldNotUse: Artists or designers who need high-quality AI image generation for creative or portfolio work. Canva AI's image quality doesn't match Midjourney or DALL-E 3 for standalone AI art. Canva is for templates and quick design — not serious AI image generation.

name: QuillBot
tagline: The AI paraphrasing and rewriting tool
bestFor: Paraphrasing, rewriting, and summarizing existing content
pros: Best paraphrasing tool available — genuinely good at rewriting
cons: Not an AI writing assistant — it rewrites, it doesn't create
Free tier limited to 125 words per paraphrase
Can produce awkward phrasing on complex sentences
Grammar checker is weaker than Grammarly
notGoodAt: Creating original content from scratch. QuillBot rewrites — if you have nothing to start with, you need ChatGPT or Claude first.

name: Riverside.fm
tagline: Studio-quality podcast and video recording in your browser
bestFor: Podcast recording, remote interviews, creators who need studio-quality audio without a studio
pros: Records locally — studio-quality audio even with bad internet
Separate tracks per speaker, essential for clean editing
AI transcription included on all paid plans
Progressive uploads so you never lose a recording
Video and audio in the same session
cons: It's a recorder, not an editor — limited post-production tools
More expensive than Zoom for basic calls
No AI avatar or synthetic content features
notGoodAt: Post-production editing. Riverside records exceptionally well but anything beyond basic cuts requires exporting to Descript or a proper video editor. Don't buy Riverside expecting an all-in-one suite.

name: Gamma
tagline: AI-powered presentations without the slide-by-slide grind
bestFor: Creating presentations, pitch decks, and documents from a prompt in minutes
pros: Generates an entire presentation from a single text prompt
Modern templates that don't look like PowerPoint from 2012
Easy to edit sections without starting over
Embeds cleanly in Notion and docs
Generous free tier with enough to evaluate properly
cons: Less fine-grained control than PowerPoint or Google Slides
Limited custom branding on free plan
Not great for complex data visualizations
notGoodAt: Pixel-perfect, brand-consistent presentations for enterprise pitches where design precision matters. For those, you still need PowerPoint or Keynote. Gamma's strength is speed for good-enough — not perfection.

name: ElevenLabs
tagline: The most realistic AI voice generation and voice cloning platform
bestFor: AI voiceovers, podcast narration, video narration, and voice cloning for creators
pros: Most realistic AI voices available — genuinely hard to tell from humans
Voice cloning from just a few minutes of audio
cons: Free tier has very strict monthly character limits
Voice cloning raises serious ethical and consent issues
Real-time voice conversion is still improving
Not a full audio production suite — just voice generation
notGoodAt: Full audio production, podcast editing, or anything beyond voice generation. ElevenLabs creates voices — you still need Descript or a DAW for everything else in your audio workflow.

name: Loom
tagline: Record and share quick video messages for async team communication
bestFor: Async team communication, quick video walkthroughs, and remote team updates
pros: Screen + webcam recording with one click
Instant shareable link — no uploading needed
cons: Basic editing only — not a video editor
Getting more expensive since Atlassian acquisition
notGoodAt: Polished, edited video content. Loom is for quick-and-async, not professional production. For anything you'd actually publish publicly, you need Descript or a proper editor.
